original_hypothesis.title: Forwarder Accepts Arbitrary Domain Separator Enabling Cross-Contract/Cross-Chain Signature Replay
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The Forwarder.execute() accepts domainSeparator as an external parameter and passes it directly to _verifySig() without validating it against the Forwarder's own contract address or chain. Any valid EIP-712 signature from the user on any contract with the same ForwardRequest struct layout can be replayed on this Forwarder by passing the original contract's domain separator.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 domain separators exist specifically to prevent cross-contract replay. By accepting arbitrary domain separators, this protection is entirely bypassed. The project targets Ethereum and Polygon, making cross-chain replay a concrete threat for multi-chain users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: domainSeparator parameter used directly in digest computation without validation against contract's own domain; Forwarder has no EIP-712 domain separator to compare against

preconditions: User has signed a ForwardRequest on another Forwarder instance or chain with matching struct format
Target EURF address matches req.to on the target Forwarder
User nonce on target Forwarder matches the signed nonce (e.g., both 0 for new users)
User has sufficient balance on target chain for transfer + fees

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bytes32 digest = keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19\x01", domainSeparator, ...)) uses caller-supplied domainSeparator
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No comparison of domainSeparator against a contract-derived value; Forwarder does not inherit EIP712 and has no own domain separator
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing: require(domainSeparator == _computeDomainSeparator()) or inheriting EIP-712 for domain binding

analysis_questions: Does the project deploy Forwarders at the same address on Ethereum and Polygon?
Can signatures from OpenZeppelin's MinimalForwarder (same struct) be replayed here?
What is the nonce state of users on secondary Forwarder deployments?
validation_requirements: Verify Forwarder has no internal domain separator computation
Test cross-chain replay scenario with matching EURF addresses
Test replay from a different Forwarder instance on same chain

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User signs ForwardRequest on Polygon Forwarder (nonce=0, to=EURF_polygon, transfer 1000 EURF to recipient). Same user has never used Ethereum Forwarder (nonce=0). If EURF addresses match across chains, attacker submits the same signature to Ethereum Forwarder with Polygon's domain separator. Signature verifies because domainSeparator is unchecked. Transfer of 1000 EURF executes on Ethereum without user intent.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unauthorized transfers of user tokens on secondary chain/Forwarder. Full balance at risk per replayed transaction.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Tokens transferred to attacker-controlled recipient are unrecoverable without forceTransfer

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for cross-contract or cross-chain signature replay with different domain separators

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction, after obtaining signature from another context
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Replay detectable by monitoring nonce consumption on multiple Forwarders

validation_result.mechanism: unvalidated domain separator enables cross-contract/cross-chain signature replay
validation_result.impact_summary: The Forwarder accepts any caller-supplied domainSeparator without validation, completely bypassing EIP-712 cross-contract replay protection. A valid signature from any other Forwarder or compatible contract can be replayed on this Forwarder if nonce and target address conditions align.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The Forwarder.execute() function accepts domainSeparator as a bytes32 parameter from the caller. This value flows directly into _verifySig() where it is used in the EIP-712 digest computation: `keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19\x01", domainSeparator, keccak256(_getEncoded(...))))`. At no point is this domainSeparator validated against a contract-derived value. The Forwarder contract does NOT inherit EIP712Upgradeable, does NOT compute its own domain separator (containing address(this) and block.chainid), and does NOT store any reference domain separator for comparison. This means an attacker who obtains a valid ForwardRequest signature from any other contract or chain with the same struct layout can replay it on this Forwarder by simply passing the original domain separator. The only constraints are: (1) nonce alignment (nonces[req.from] must match req.nonce), (2) req.to must equal _eurfAddress, and (3) the requestTypeHash must be registered. For users who have never interacted with the target Forwarder (nonce=0), cross-chain replay is straightforward if the EURF token address matches across chains.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal references Forwarder.sol lines 75-98 for the execute function, but the actual vulnerable code is in _verifySig at lines ~113-122. The execute function at lines ~86-112 passes domainSeparator through without any validation. The Forwarder class declaration at line 33 shows it only inherits OwnableUpgradeable, not EIP712Upgradeable. There is no domain separator computation or storage anywhere in the contract. The initialize function (lines ~68-78) sets up _eurfAddress and registers a request type but creates no domain binding.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The domainSeparator parameter is used directly in the EIP-712 digest computation without any validation. The Forwarder has no internal domain separator to compare against. Any caller-supplied domain separator is accepted, meaning a signature produced for a different contract or chain can be replayed here.
Entry point that accepts arbitrary domainSeparator from any external caller and passes it unchecked to _verifySig.
The Forwarder only inherits OwnableUpgradeable - it does NOT inherit EIP712Upgradeable and therefore has no mechanism to compute or store its own EIP-712 domain separator for validation.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls Forwarder.execute(req, foreignDomainSeparator, requestTypeHash, suffixData, sig)
2. _verifyNonce(req) checks nonces[req.from] == req.nonce (passes if user never used this Forwarder, nonce=0)
3. _verifySig(req, foreignDomainSeparator, ...) computes digest using the foreign domain separator
4. digest.recover(sig) == req.from passes because signature was validly signed with that foreign domain separator
6. req.to.call(...) executes the transfer on EURF token
7. _eurf.payGaslessBasefee(req.from, msg.sender) charges the victim gasless fee
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path starts at execute() (line 86) where domainSeparator is accepted as a parameter. It flows to _verifySig() (line 113) where it's used in the digest at line 120: `keccak256(abi.encodePacked("\x19\x01", domainSeparator, ...))`. Since there is no `require(domainSeparator == expectedDomainSeparator)` check anywhere in the contract (and no expectedDomainSeparator exists), any domain separator that produces a valid signature from req.from will pass. The signature then authorizes the transfer execution at line 107: `req.to.call{gas: req.gas, value: req.value}(abi.encodePacked(req.data, req.from))`.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: hardhat.config.js shows network configurations for sepolia and amoy (Polygon testnet), confirming multi-chain deployment intent. deployToken.js deploys EURFToken behind UUPS proxy. No Forwarder deployment script is provided, but the multi-chain config makes cross-chain replay scenarios realistic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is viable under these conditions: (1) User signs a ForwardRequest on Forwarder-A (e.g., Polygon) with nonce=0, to=EURF_address, data=transfer(recipient, amount). (2) Attacker obtains this signature (it was broadcast publicly as a meta-tx). (3) If EURF is deployed at the same address on another chain (Ethereum), and the user has never used Forwarder-B on that chain (nonce=0), the attacker submits the same (req, domainSeparator_of_ForwarderA, requestTypeHash, suffixData, sig) to Forwarder-B. (4) _verifySig passes because the digest is computed with Forwarder-A's domain separator (which the attacker provides), and the signature was originally signed over that exact digest. (5) The transfer executes on the target chain without user's intent. Even on the SAME chain, if two Forwarder instances exist (e.g., during migration), signatures from one can be replayed on the other.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Critical impact: unauthorized token transfers from users who have signed ForwardRequests in other contexts. The full transfer amount plus gasless basefee can be stolen per replayed transaction. Any user who has interacted with any compatible Forwarder and has matching nonce state on the target Forwarder is at risk. For new users on multiple chains (nonce=0 everywhere), the attack window is immediately available.
recommended_mitigations: Have the Forwarder inherit EIP712Upgradeable and compute its own domain separator during initialization, then validate: require(domainSeparator == _domainSeparatorV4(), 'invalid domain separator')
Alternatively, remove domainSeparator as a parameter entirely and use a contract-computed domain separator internally in _verifySig
If the flexible domain separator design is intentional (e.g., for GSN compatibility), document this as an accepted risk and ensure EURF addresses differ across chains to prevent cross-chain replay
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy two Forwarder instances (ForwarderA, ForwarderB) pointing to same EURF token. 2. User signs ForwardRequest with ForwarderA's hypothetical domain separator (nonce=0, transfer 1000 EURF to recipient). 3. Execute on ForwarderA - succeeds, nonce advances to 1. 4. User has nonce=0 on ForwarderB. 5. Attacker calls ForwarderB.execute() with same (req with nonce=0, ForwarderA's domainSeparator, sig). 6. Verify signature passes and transfer executes on ForwarderB without user's authorization on that instance.
uncertainties: Whether EURF token addresses are identical across chains (deterministic deployment) - if they differ, req.to check would fail for cross-chain replay
Whether multiple Forwarder instances exist on the same chain
The practical availability of valid signatures from other contexts (meta-tx signatures are typically broadcast publicly to relayers)
additional_testing_needed: Test cross-instance replay with two Forwarders on same chain pointing to same EURF
Test cross-chain replay if EURF addresses match
Verify whether any off-chain relayer infrastructure enforces domain separator validation that the contract does not
